title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
text: ATC hierarchy five-level tree classifies every drug broad anatomical group individual chemical substance. Navigating tree essential pharmacoepidemiology — need answer questions like: “drugs belong beta-blocker class?” “children statin subclass?” “many Level 5 substances nervous system group?” vignette shows traverse, filter, visualise hierarchy using atcddd.
code: library(atcddd) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)

title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
text: start bundled snapshot everything works offline:
code: codes_path <- system.file("extdata", "WHO_ATC_codes_2026-07-14.csv",                           package = "atcddd") ddd_path   <- system.file("extdata", "WHO_ATC_DDD_2026-07-14.csv",                           package = "atcddd")  atc_codes <- readr::read_csv(codes_path, show_col_types = FALSE) atc_ddd   <- readr::read_csv(ddd_path, show_col_types = FALSE)  # Add level and parent columns using exported functions atc_codes <- atc_codes %>%   mutate(     level       = atc_level(atc_code),     parent_code = atc_parent(atc_code)   )  cat(sprintf("Loaded %d codes across 5 levels", nrow(atc_codes))) #> Loaded 6982 codes across 5 levels

title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
text: five levels form strict tree:
code: Level 1:  C                   (1 char)      Cardiovascular system Level 2:  C10                 (3 chars)     Lipid modifying agents Level 3:  C10A                (4 chars)     Lipid modifying agents, plain Level 4:  C10AA               (5 chars)     HMG CoA reductase inhibitors Level 5:  C10AA01             (7 chars)     simvastatin

title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
text: Level 5 (individual substances) dominates — anatomical group fans hundreds specific drugs.
code: atc_codes %>%   count(level) %>%   mutate(     pct   = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1),     label = case_when(       level == 1 ~ "Anatomical main group",       level == 2 ~ "Therapeutic subgroup",       level == 3 ~ "Pharmacological subgroup",       level == 4 ~ "Chemical subgroup",       level == 5 ~ "Chemical substance"     )   ) %>%   arrange(level) %>%   select(label, n, pct) #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   label                        n   pct #>   <chr>                    <int> <dbl> #> 1 Therapeutic subgroup        94   1.3 #> 2 Pharmacological subgroup   271   3.9 #> 3 Chemical subgroup          939  13.4 #> 4 Chemical substance        5678  81.3

what: Find the Full Ancestry of a Drug
title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
text: ancestry trace valuable : - Study inclusion criteria — “analgesics except opioids” - Pharmacovigilance — signal detection within therapeutic class - Drug utilisation — aggregating consumption class
code: trace_ancestry <- function(code) {   parts <- list()   current <- code   while (!is.na(current) && current != "") {     row <- atc_codes %>% filter(atc_code == current)     if (nrow(row) == 0) break     parts[[length(parts) + 1]] <- row     current <- atc_parent(current)   }   bind_rows(rev(parts)) %>%     select(atc_code, atc_name, level) }  # Trace paracetamol from Level 1 down trace_ancestry("N02BE01") #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   atc_code atc_name                          level #>   <chr>    <chr>                             <int> #> 1 N02      ANALGESICS                            2 #> 2 N02B     OTHER ANALGESICS AND ANTIPYRETICS     3 #> 3 N02BE    Anilides                              4 #> 4 N02BE01  paracetamol                           5

title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
text: --date data, use get_atc_hierarchy(): get_atc_hierarchy() adds has_children column — TRUE code sub-classifications, useful building interactive tree widgets.
code: # Get the complete nervous-system hierarchy (live) ns_tree <- get_atc_hierarchy("N", max_levels = 5)  # This returns the same enriched structure ns_tree %>%   select(atc_code, atc_name, level, parent_code, has_children) %>%   head(10)

text: Groups J (antiinfectives), N (nervous system), (alimentary tract) largest — reflecting decades drug development therapeutic areas.

what: Recipe 2: Build a Complete Class-to-Drug Map
title: Navigating the ATC Hierarchy
code: # Map: every Level 4 chemical class → all Level 5 substances in it class_to_drug <- atc_codes %>%   filter(level == 5) %>%   mutate(chemical_class = substr(atc_code, 1, 5)) %>%   select(chemical_class, substance_code = atc_code, substance_name = atc_name)  # Which chemical classes contain "fluoroquinolone"? atc_codes %>%   filter(level == 4, grepl("fluoroquinolone", atc_name, ignore.case = TRUE)) %>%   inner_join(class_to_drug, by = c("atc_code" = "chemical_class")) %>%   select(chemical_class = atc_code, class_name = atc_name,          substance_code, substance_name) #> # A tibble: 33 × 4 #>    chemical_class class_name       substance_code substance_name #>    <chr>          <chr>            <chr>          <chr>          #>  1 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA01        ofloxacin      #>  2 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA02        ciprofloxacin  #>  3 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA03        pefloxacin     #>  4 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA04        enoxacin       #>  5 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA05        temafloxacin   #>  6 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA06        norfloxacin    #>  7 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA07        lomefloxacin   #>  8 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA08        fleroxacin     #>  9 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA09        sparfloxacin   #> 10 J01MA          Fluoroquinolones J01MA10        rufloxacin     #> # ℹ 23 more rows

title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: prescription records — pharmacy database, claims file, electronic health record (EHR) extracts — need answer pharmacoepidemiology question: much drug consumed? challenge prescriptions come many forms: Different strengths — 20 mg vs. 40 mg tablets drug Different quantities — 30 tablets one patient, 90 another Different units — milligrams, grams, micrograms, international units Different routes — oral, parenteral, rectal, transdermal Defined Daily Dose (DDD) solves . expressing every prescription common unit — assumed average maintenance dose per day adult — can meaningfully aggregate compare drug utilisation. vignette walks practical workflow: Computing DDDs raw prescription records Aggregating DDD per 1000 inhabitants per day () Handling different administration routes Dealing drugs lack DDDs Building complete analysis pipeline
code: library(atcddd) library(dplyr)

title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: package bundles ATC/DDD snapshot works completely offline. load inspect reference data computing. ⚠️ code vignette evaluates offline — internet connection required.
code: codes_path <- system.file("extdata", "WHO_ATC_codes_2026-07-14.csv",                           package = "atcddd") ddd_path   <- system.file("extdata", "WHO_ATC_DDD_2026-07-14.csv",                           package = "atcddd")  atc_codes <- readr::read_csv(codes_path, show_col_types = FALSE) atc_ddd   <- readr::read_csv(ddd_path, show_col_types = FALSE)  cat(sprintf("Bundled data: %d ATC codes, %d DDD entries",             nrow(atc_codes), nrow(atc_ddd))) #> Bundled data: 6982 ATC codes, 6218 DDD entries

what: Step-by-Step: Computing DDDs for a Single Drug
title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: Let’s start simple: one drug, one prescription. patient receives acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) 500 mg tablets, 100 tablets dispensed. output adds four columns data: Interpretation: 100 tablets 500 mg aspirin = 50 g drug substance. DDD oral aspirin 3 g, prescription corresponds 16.7 DDDs (50 g / 3 g = 16.7). function automatically converts units — noticed strength milligrams DDD grams handled conversion.
code: aspirin_rx <- data.frame(   atc_code      = "N02BA01",   quantity      = 100,   strength      = 500,   strength_unit = "mg",   adm_r         = "O" )  compute_ddd(aspirin_rx) #> # A tibble: 1 × 9 #>   atc_code quantity strength strength_unit adm_r ddd_value ddd_unit total_amount #>   <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>         <chr>     <dbl> <chr>           <dbl> #> 1 N02BA01       100      500 mg            O             3 g               50000 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: ddd_ratio <dbl>

title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: Real-world data multiple patients multiple drugs. Let’s create realistic scenario 5 prescription records 3 patients. row now shows DDD ratio alongside original prescription data. Let’s interpret results: Patient 1 received 16.7 DDDs aspirin (100 x 500 mg / 3000 mg per DDD) plus 30 DDDs atorvastatin (30 x 20 mg / 20 mg per DDD) — total 46.7 DDDs across two drugs. Patient 2 received 10 DDDs aspirin 22.5 DDDs metformin (90 x 500 mg = 45 g; DDD = 2 g; 45 / 2 = 22.5). Patient 3 received 18.7 DDDs atenolol (28 x 50 mg = 1400 mg; DDD = 75 mg; 1400 / 75 = 18.7).
code: prescriptions <- data.frame(   patient_id    = c(1, 1, 2, 2, 3),   atc_code      = c("N02BA01", "C10AA05", "N02BA01", "A10BA02", "C07AB03"),   drug_name     = c("aspirin", "atorvastatin", "aspirin", "metformin", "atenolol"),   quantity      = c(100, 30, 60, 90, 28),   strength      = c(500, 20, 500, 500, 50),   strength_unit = c("mg", "mg", "mg", "mg", "mg"),   adm_r         = c("O", "O", "O", "O", "O") )  ddd_result <- compute_ddd(prescriptions) ddd_result #> # A tibble: 5 × 11 #>   patient_id atc_code drug_name  quantity strength strength_unit adm_r ddd_value #>        <dbl> <chr>    <chr>         <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>         <chr>     <dbl> #> 1          1 N02BA01  aspirin         100      500 mg            O             3 #> 2          1 C10AA05  atorvasta…       30       20 mg            O            20 #> 3          2 N02BA01  aspirin          60      500 mg            O             3 #> 4          2 A10BA02  metformin        90      500 mg            O             2 #> 5          3 C07AB03  atenolol         28       50 mg            O            75 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: ddd_unit <chr>, total_amount <dbl>, ddd_ratio <dbl>

title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: Group patient_id see total drug utilisation per patient:
code: ddd_result %>%   group_by(patient_id) %>%   summarise(     n_prescriptions = n(),     total_ddd       = sum(ddd_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   patient_id n_prescriptions total_ddd #>        <dbl>           <int>     <dbl> #> 1          1               2      46.7 #> 2          2               2      32.5 #> 3          3               1      18.7

title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: standard population-level metric drug utilisation research. answers: average day, many people 1000 receive one DDD? =Total DDDsPopulation×Days×1000\text{} = \frac{\text{Total DDDs}}{\text{Population} \times \text{Days}} \times 1000 Interpretation: 0.65 means , average day 30-day study period, approximately 0.65 every 1000 inhabitants (1 1500) received one DDD studied medications. values let compare drug utilisation across: Regions — antibiotic use higher country B? Time periods — statin use increase policy change? Drug classes — therapeutic class highest utilisation? ⚠️ population metric, clinical one. 30 statins tells 3% population uses statin DDD day — tell whether individual patient right dose.

what: DDD Coverage in the Bundled Data
title: Computing Defined Daily Doses from Prescription Data
text: ddd_availability() function gives immediate overview anatomical groups good DDD coverage . Key observations: Groups J (antiinfectives), N (nervous system), C (cardiovascular) high DDD coverage — systemic drugs DDD well-defined. code: # Example: prescription records for 3 patients prescriptions <- tibble(   patient_id = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 3),   atc_code   = c("N02BE01", "C10AA05", "N02BE01", "A02BC01", "C10AA05"),   quantity   = c(30, 30, 60, 28, 90),       # tablets/capsules   strength   = c(500, 20, 500, 20, 40),      # mg per unit   unit       = c("mg", "mg", "mg", "mg", "mg") )  # Join with DDD data (using the oral route, adm_r = "O") ddd_ref <- atc_ddd %>%   filter(!is.na(ddd), ddd != "NA", adm_r == "O") %>%   select(atc_code, atc_name, ddd, uom) %>%   mutate(ddd_value = as.numeric(ddd))  prescriptions %>%   left_join(ddd_ref, by = "atc_code") %>%   mutate(     # Convert DDD to mg if needed     ddd_mg = case_when(       uom == "g"  ~ ddd_value * 1000,       uom == "mg" ~ ddd_value,       uom == "mcg" ~ ddd_value / 1000,       TRUE        ~ NA_real_     ),     total_mg   = quantity * strength,     n_ddd      = total_mg / ddd_mg   ) %>%   select(patient_id, atc_code, atc_name, quantity, strength,          total_mg, ddd_mg, n_ddd) #> # A tibble: 5 × 8 #>   patient_id atc_code atc_name     quantity strength total_mg ddd_mg n_ddd #>        <dbl> <chr>    <chr>           <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #> 1          1 N02BE01  paracetamol        30      500    15000   3000     5 #> 2          1 C10AA05  atorvastatin       30       20      600     20    30 #> 3          2 N02BE01  paracetamol        60      500    30000   3000    10 #> 4          3 A02BC01  omeprazole         28       20      560     20    28 #> 5          3 C10AA05  atorvastatin       90       40     3600     20   180

code: # Join DDD data with Level 4 classification atc_ddd %>%   filter(!is.na(ddd), ddd != "NA", adm_r == "O") %>%   mutate(     ddd_value  = as.numeric(ddd),     class_code = substr(atc_code, 1, 5)   # Level 4 chemical class   ) %>%   group_by(class_code) %>%   summarise(     n_drugs        = n_distinct(atc_code),     mean_ddd       = mean(ddd_value, na.rm = TRUE),     median_ddd     = median(ddd_value, na.rm = TRUE),     min_ddd        = min(ddd_value, na.rm = TRUE),     max_ddd        = max(ddd_value, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups        = "drop"   ) %>%   left_join(     atc_codes %>% filter(nchar(atc_code) == 5) %>% select(atc_code, class_name = atc_name),     by = c("class_code" = "atc_code")   ) %>%   filter(n_drugs >= 3) %>%   arrange(desc(mean_ddd)) %>%   select(class_code, class_name, n_drugs, mean_ddd, median_ddd) %>%   head(10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>    class_code class_name                             n_drugs mean_ddd median_ddd #>    <chr>      <chr>                                    <int>    <dbl>      <dbl> #>  1 C04AA      2-amino-1-phenylethanol derivatives          3     55         60   #>  2 N07CA      Antivertigo preparations                     4     53.5       57   #>  3 A04AD      Other antiemetics                            4     45.8        9   #>  4 L01EE      Mitogen-activated protein kinase (MEK…       3     45.7       45   #>  5 N05AE      Indole derivatives                           5     41.2       50   #>  6 L01EB      Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGF…       6     40.9       42.5 #>  7 B03AB      Iron trivalent, oral preparations            6     38.5       30.4 #>  8 R05DB      Other cough suppressants                    15     37.5       25   #>  9 C03DA      Aldosterone antagonists                      3     35.8       37.5 #> 10 C01DA      Organic nitrates                             7     35.0       30

text: DDDs enable apples--apples comparisons — normalise drug consumption across different strengths, package sizes, formulations. Match route administration (adm_r) — drug can different DDDs oral, parenteral, rectal routes. Convert units carefully — DDDs come g, mg, mcg, TU, MU, units. Always convert common unit computing total DDDs. Document missing DDDs — 40% study drugs lack DDDs, report . Transparency coverage important perfect coverage. DDD ≠ clinical dose — use DDD population-level comparisons, individual patient management.

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: atcddd package provides comprehensive tools working World Health Organization’s Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) Classification System Defined Daily Dose (DDD) values. Whether need crawl latest data, validate ATC codes, navigate five-level drug hierarchy, compute DDD metrics prescription records — atcddd gives tidy, reproducible workflows R.

what: What is the ATC Classification System?
title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: ATC system divides drugs five levels increasing specificity:

what: What is the Defined Daily Dose (DDD)?
title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: DDD assumed average maintenance dose per day drug used main indication adults. unit measurement, recommended dose. DDDs enable researchers compare drug utilisation across populations, regions, time periods — even drugs come different strengths package sizes.

title: Getting Started with atcddd
code: library(atcddd) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: ATC system starts 14 single-letter groups. Use atc_roots_default() list :
code: roots <- atc_roots_default() roots #>  [1] "A" "B" "C" "D" "G" "H" "J" "L" "M" "N" "P" "R" "S" "V"

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: is_valid_atc_code() checks whether string conforms official ATC format:
code: # Valid codes at each level is_valid_atc_code("N")          # Level 1 — TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_valid_atc_code("N02")        # Level 2 — TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_valid_atc_code("N02BE01")    # Level 5 — TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # Invalid codes is_valid_atc_code("n02be01")    # lowercase — FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_valid_atc_code("N02-BE01")   # hyphens  — FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_valid_atc_code("N02BE011")   # too long — FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_valid_atc_code("ZZZ")        # bad pattern — FALSE #> [1] FALSE

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: get_atc_data() function provides clean interface database:
code: # Get full data for aspirin aspirin <- get_atc_data("N02BA01") aspirin  # Get all analgesics with their sub-classifications analgesics <- get_atc_data("N02", include_children = TRUE) head(analgesics, 10)  # Get multiple groups at once cns_cv <- get_atc_data(c("N", "C")) cns_cv

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: control, use atc_crawl() directly:
code: # Crawl dermatological drugs (ATC group D) res <- atc_crawl(   roots     = "D",   rate      = 0.5,   progress  = TRUE,   max_codes = 100 )  # The result is a list with two tibbles str(res, max.level = 1)  # Codes table: atc_code + atc_name head(res$codes, 10)  # DDD table: source_code, atc_code, atc_name, ddd, uom, adm_r, note head(res$ddd, 5)

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: get_atc_hierarchy() function returns full parent-child tree: Column descriptions: parent_code — code one level hierarchy (NA Level 1) has_children — TRUE node sub-classifications level — hierarchy depth (1-5)
code: # Get complete hierarchy under analgesics (N02) tree <- get_atc_hierarchy("N02")  tree %>%   select(atc_code, atc_name, level, parent_code, has_children) %>%   head(10)

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: drugs DDD values — expected. assign DDDs several categories medicines. Understanding prevents false conclusions NA values appear data.
code: # Systemic antibiotics — most WILL have DDD values abx <- atc_crawl(roots = "J01AA", rate = 0.5, max_codes = 30) cat("Systemic antibiotics with DDD:",     sum(!is.na(abx$ddd$ddd)), "/", nrow(abx$ddd))  # Topical dermatologicals — most WON'T have DDD values derm <- atc_crawl(roots = "D01", rate = 0.5, max_codes = 50) cat("Topical dermatologicals with DDD:",     sum(!is.na(derm$ddd$ddd)), "/", nrow(derm$ddd))

title: Getting Started with atcddd
code: res <- atc_crawl(roots = "D", max_codes = 50) paths <- atc_write_csv(res, dir = "output", stamp = TRUE) # Creates: #   output/WHO_ATC_codes_2025-07-14.csv #   output/WHO_ATC_DDD_2025-07-14.csv

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: Use manifests document exactly version data analysis used — critical reproducible pharmacoepidemiology.
code: # Each manifest includes SHA256 checksums for every file atc_write_manifest(paths) # Creates: output/MANIFEST.csv

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: package bundles ATC/DDD snapshot data can loaded without internet connection:
code: # Paths to bundled CSV data codes_path <- system.file("extdata", "WHO_ATC_codes_2026-07-14.csv",                           package = "atcddd") ddd_path   <- system.file("extdata", "WHO_ATC_DDD_2026-07-14.csv",                           package = "atcddd")  # Load into R atc_codes <- readr::read_csv(codes_path, show_col_types = FALSE) atc_ddd   <- readr::read_csv(ddd_path, show_col_types = FALSE)  # Quick look cat(sprintf("ATC codes table: %d rows\n", nrow(atc_codes))) #> ATC codes table: 6982 rows cat(sprintf("ATC DDD table:   %d rows\n", nrow(atc_ddd))) #> ATC DDD table:   6218 rows  head(atc_codes, 5) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>   atc_code atc_name                    #>   <chr>    <chr>                       #> 1 A01      STOMATOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS #> 2 A01A     STOMATOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS #> 3 A01AA    Caries prophylactic agents  #> 4 A01AA01  sodium fluoride             #> 5 A01AA02  sodium monofluorophosphate

title: Getting Started with atcddd
code: # Find all codes containing "paracetamol" atc_codes %>%   filter(grepl("paracetamol", atc_name, ignore.case = TRUE)) #> # A tibble: 8 × 2 #>   atc_code atc_name                                      #>   <chr>    <chr>                                         #> 1 N02AJ01  dihydrocodeine and paracetamol                #> 2 N02AJ06  codeine and paracetamol                       #> 3 N02AJ13  tramadol and paracetamol                      #> 4 N02AJ17  oxycodone and paracetamol                     #> 5 N02AJ22  hydrocodone and paracetamol                   #> 6 N02BE01  paracetamol                                   #> 7 N02BE51  paracetamol, combinations excl. psycholeptics #> 8 N02BE71  paracetamol, combinations with psycholeptics  # Find all Level 2 therapeutic subgroups atc_codes %>%   filter(nchar(atc_code) == 3) %>%   head(10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 2 #>    atc_code atc_name                                                         #>    <chr>    <chr>                                                            #>  1 A01      STOMATOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS                                      #>  2 A02      DRUGS FOR ACID RELATED DISORDERS                                 #>  3 A03      DRUGS FOR FUNCTIONAL GASTROINTESTINAL DISORDERS                  #>  4 A04      ANTIEMETICS AND ANTINAUSEANTS                                    #>  5 A05      BILE AND LIVER THERAPY                                           #>  6 A06      DRUGS FOR CONSTIPATION                                           #>  7 A07      ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS #>  8 A08      ANTIOBESITY PREPARATIONS, EXCL. DIET PRODUCTS                    #>  9 A09      DIGESTIVES, INCL. ENZYMES                                        #> 10 A10      DRUGS USED IN DIABETES

what: Analysis Example: DDD Availability by Route
title: Getting Started with atcddd
code: # Analyse DDD patterns across administration routes atc_ddd %>%   filter(!is.na(adm_r) & adm_r != "NA" & adm_r != "") %>%   count(adm_r, sort = TRUE) %>%   mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%   head(10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>    adm_r              n   pct #>    <chr>          <int> <dbl> #>  1 O               1670  60.5 #>  2 P                817  29.6 #>  3 R                 79   2.9 #>  4 N                 38   1.4 #>  5 V                 37   1.3 #>  6 Inhal.solution    25   0.9 #>  7 Inhal.aerosol     23   0.8 #>  8 Inhal.powder      23   0.8 #>  9 SL                16   0.6 #> 10 TD                14   0.5

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: pyramid shape characteristic — one Level 1 root produces many Level 5 substances, reflecting drug classes branch specific chemical entities.
code:
# Classify codes by level atc_codes %>%   mutate(     code_len = nchar(atc_code),     level = case_when(       code_len == 1 ~ "Level 1
(Anatomical)",       code_len == 3 ~ "Level 2
(Therapeutic)",       code_len == 4 ~ "Level 3
(Pharmacological)",       code_len == 5 ~ "Level 4
(Chemical)",       code_len == 7 ~ "Level 5
(Substance)",       TRUE         ~ "Other"     )   ) %>%   filter(level != "Other") %>%   count(level) %>%   mutate(level = factor(level, levels = c(     "Level 1
(Anatomical)", "Level 2
(Therapeutic)",     "Level 3
(Pharmacological)", "Level 4
(Chemical)",     "Level 5
(Substance)"   ))) %>%   ggplot(aes(x = level, y = n, fill = level)) +   geom_col(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +   geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(     title    = "Distribution of ATC Codes by Hierarchy Level",     subtitle = paste("Bundled WHO data snapshot:", nrow(atc_codes), "total codes"),     x        = NULL,     y        = "Number of Codes",     caption  = "Source: WHO ATC/DDD Index"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(     legend.position = "none",     plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),     plot.subtitle   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "grey40"),     panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()   )

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: Always use appropriate delays respect server resources:
code: # Conservative: 1 request per second atc_crawl(rate = 1.0)  # Minimum delay for large crawls: 0.5 seconds atc_crawl(rate = 0.5)

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: package caches HTTP responses automatically via memoise. Repeated requests URL return cached data instantly: Cache location (platform-dependent): rappdirs::user_cache_dir("atcddd")
code: # First run: fetches from WHO (slow) res1 <- atc_crawl(roots = "D", max_codes = 20)  # Second run: reads from cache (fast) res2 <- atc_crawl(roots = "D", max_codes = 20)

title: Getting Started with atcddd
text: published analysis, generate checksum manifest reviewers can verify data:
code: res   <- atc_crawl(roots = c("C", "N")) paths <- atc_write_csv(res, dir = "data", stamp = TRUE) atc_write_manifest(paths)

title: Reply to Laura de Jong
text: Hi Laura, worries — Laura perfectly clear first name ! thank kind words, ’m glad atcddd looks useful clinical trials work. ’re exactly right get_atc_data("N02BA01") — queries ATC/DDD index website retrieves classification info DDD data code. ’s good news: ’re asking already exists package! don’t need separate get_atc_code() function several functions go drug name → ATC code: resolve_atc("aspirin") — main one. Give drug name, get back ATC code, official name, DDD information. works fully offline bundled database, ’s instant needs internet. also knows common brand names (e.g., “lipitor” → atorvastatin, “tylenol” → paracetamol). resolve_batch(c("aspirin", "metformin", "ibuprofen")) — thing whole vector drug names , perfect column medications code. search_drug("statin") — exploratory: searches drug names substring returns matches, ranked quality. fuzzy_match_drug("asprin") — handles typos misspellings using edit distance (“acetominophen” still finds paracetamol). practice: ’s also atc_add_synonym() study-specific brand names want register resolve correctly. getting ATC codes free-text clinical notes, ’s atc_from_text(). Hope helps! please don’t hesitate email open GitHub issue run anything — GitHub expertise needed, quick email works just well. Cheers, Huynh

what: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
title: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
text: Scans unstructured clinical text drug names resolves ATC codes. Supports exact name matching, synonym (brand/common name) lookup, optional fuzzy matching misspellings. Multi-word drug names (e.g. "acetylsalicylic acid", "sodium fluoride") detected bigram tokenisation. Inspired ab_from_text() AMR package, covering full ATC/DDD index rather antimicrobials alone.

title: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
code: atc_from_text(text, max_results = 5, fuzzy = TRUE)

title: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
text: text Character scalar; free-text clinical notes "Patient received 500 mg aspirin PO TID" "atorvastatin 20 mg daily". max_results Integer; maximum number unique drugs return. Default 5. fuzzy Logical; TRUE, use Levenshtein (edit-distance) matching drug names found exact synonym lookup. Default TRUE.

title: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
text: tibble max_results rows following columns: drug_name Character; official drug name ATC database (e.g. "acetylsalicylic acid"). atc_code Character; resolved ATC code (e.g. "N02BA01"). match Character; text span input triggered match, preserving original casing (e.g. "Aspirin"). match_type Character; match made: "exact", "synonym", "fuzzy". confidence Character; heuristic quality rating: "high" (exact synonym match), "medium" (fuzzy, edit distance <= 2), "low" (fuzzy, edit distance 3). ddd Character; Defined Daily Dose value drug (preferring oral route), NA available. uom Character; unit DDD value (e.g. "g", "mg"), NA available. Returns zero-row tibble drugs detected input empty/NA.

title: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
text: Algorithm Pre-processing. input text normalised lowercase. Punctuation characters (commas, semicolons, periods, parentheses, etc.) replaced spaces tokens like "aspirin," become "aspirin". Internal hyphens preserved (e.g. "co-trimoxazole"). Tokenisation. cleaned text split whitespace individual words. Two-word sequences (bigrams) also generated multi-word drug names missed. Filtering. Tokens represent dosing information (e.g. "500mg", "20mg/kg"), pure numbers, short tokens (<= 2 characters) excluded matching. Overly generic chemical terms ("acid", "sodium", "chloride", etc.) allowed part bigram. Bigram matching (Phase 1). consecutive word pair survives filtering looked via search_drug() (exact name synonym check). fuzzy = TRUE exact/synonym match found, fuzzy_match_drug() tried pair. Words consumed successful bigram match re-processed unigrams. Unigram matching (Phase 2). Remaining words matched individually, first via search_drug() (fuzzy = TRUE) via fuzzy_match_drug(). De-duplication ranking. Results de-duplicated ATC code (keeping highest-confidence match). final set ranked confidence (high > medium > low) truncated max_results.

title: Extract drug names from free-text clinical notes — atc_from_text
text: high token exactly matches drug name curated synonym. Considered reliable. medium token fuzzy-matches Levenshtein distance 1 2 — likely minor typo. low token fuzzy-matches Levenshtein distance 3 — speculative. Manual verification recommended.

title: Determine the ATC hierarchy level of a code — atc_level

title: Compute a Checksum Manifest for Output Files — atc_manifest
text: SHA256 checksums provide unique fingerprint file. Even minor changes file contents result completely different checksums, making ideal verifying data integrity documenting exact versions used analyses.

title: Compute a Checksum Manifest for Output Files — atc_manifest
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate manifest for crawled data res <- atc_crawl(roots = "D", max_codes = 50) paths <- atc_write_csv(res, dir = "data") manifest <- atc_manifest(paths) print(manifest)  # Check file integrity later current_manifest <- atc_manifest(paths) identical(manifest, current_manifest)  # TRUE if files unchanged } # }

what: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
title: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
text: Converts data frame prescription-level drug utilisation data Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) per drug per prescription. row represents one prescription line (one drug one patient). function looks DDD ATC code, accounting route administration, computes number DDDs dispensed.

title: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
code: compute_ddd(x, adm_r = "O", ...)

title: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
text: x data frame following columns: atc_code Character vector ATC codes (Level 5, e.g. "N02BE01"). quantity Numeric; number units administered (tablets, ml, etc.). strength Numeric; amount per unit (mg per tablet, mg per ml, etc.). strength_unit Optional character; unit strength. Defaults "mg" supplied. adm_r Optional character; route administration per row. absent, global adm_r parameter used. additional columns (e.g. patient_id, prescription_date) preserved output. adm_r Default administration route rows without per-row adm_r column. Default "O" (oral). ... Reserved future extensions. Currently unused.

title: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
text: tibble inheriting columns x plus: ddd_value DDD value looked database, unit (e.g. g, mg). ddd_unit unit DDD value. total_amount total amount drug: quantity * strength, expressed strength_unit. ddd_ratio number DDDs: total_amount / ddd_value, properly unit-converted.

title: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
text: DDD Lookup Logic Try match atc_code + adm_r (route-specific DDD). route-specific DDD exists, fall back non-NA DDD ATC code. DDD found , result NA warning issued listing codes missing DDDs. Unit Conversion function automatically converts compatible units prescription strength_unit differs DDD unit. Supported conversions: Mass: g, mg, mcg (grams) Units: U, TU, MU (units, thousand units, million units) Volume: ml (exact match ) Incompatible unit pairs (e.g. mg vs MU) produce NA warning.

title: Convert prescription data into Defined Daily Doses (DDDs) — compute_ddd
code: # \donttest{ prescriptions <- data.frame(   patient_id    = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   atc_code      = c("N02BA01", "C10AA05", "N02BA01", "A10BA02"),   quantity      = c(100, 30, 60, 90),   strength      = c(500, 20, 500, 500),   strength_unit = c("mg", "mg", "mg", "mg"),   adm_r         = c("O", "O", "O", "O") ) compute_ddd(prescriptions) #> # A tibble: 4 × 10 #>   patient_id atc_code quantity strength strength_unit adm_r ddd_value ddd_unit #>        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>         <chr>     <dbl> <chr>    #> 1          1 N02BA01       100      500 mg            O             3 g        #> 2          1 C10AA05        30       20 mg            O            20 mg       #> 3          2 N02BA01        60      500 mg            O             3 g        #> 4          3 A10BA02        90      500 mg            O             2 g        #> # ℹ 2 more variables: total_amount <dbl>, ddd_ratio <dbl>  # Without strength_unit column (defaults to "mg") df <- data.frame(   atc_code = c("N02BA01", "C10AA05"),   quantity = c(100, 30),   strength = c(500, 20) ) compute_ddd(df) #> # A tibble: 2 × 9 #>   atc_code quantity strength strength_unit adm_r ddd_value ddd_unit total_amount #>   <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>         <chr>     <dbl> <chr>           <dbl> #> 1 N02BA01       100      500 mg            O             3 g               50000 #> 2 C10AA05        30       20 mg            O            20 mg                600 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: ddd_ratio <dbl> # }

what: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
title: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
text: Computes standard drug utilisation metric: number Defined Daily Doses per 1000 inhabitants per day. normalises drug consumption across different population sizes observation periods, enabling fair comparisons populations time periods.

title: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
code: compute_did(ddd_data, population, days)

title: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
text: ddd_data data frame containing least ddd_ratio column, typically output compute_ddd. population Integer; study population size. days Integer; number days study period.

title: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
text: tibble columns: total_ddd Sum ddd_ratio values. DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day. population Study population size. days Number days study period.

title: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
text: formula : $$= \frac{\sum(ddd\_ratio)}{population \times days} \times 1000$$

title: Compute DDDs per 1000 inhabitants per day (DID) — compute_did
code: # \donttest{ prescriptions <- data.frame(   atc_code      = c("N02BA01", "C10AA05", "A10BA02"),   quantity      = c(100, 30, 90),   strength      = c(500, 20, 500),   strength_unit = c("mg", "mg", "mg") ) ddd_result <- compute_ddd(prescriptions) compute_did(ddd_result, population = 10000, days = 30) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   total_ddd   did population  days #>       <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl> #> 1      69.2 0.231      10000    30 # }

title: Get ATC Classification Data from WHO Database — get_atc_data

title: Get ATC Classification Data from WHO Database — get_atc_data

title: Validate ATC Code Format — is_valid_atc_code
text: Checks whether one strings conform official ATC code format. function accepts character vectors validates element independently five recognised level patterns. Valid patterns (uppercase): Level 1: ^[-Z]$ Level 2: ^[-Z][0-9]{2}$ Level 3: ^[-Z][0-9]{2}[-Z]$ Level 4: ^[-Z][0-9]{2}[-Z]{2}$ Level 5: ^[-Z][0-9]{2}[-Z]{2}[0-9]{2}$

title: Resolve a drug name to its ATC code with hybrid local/live lookup — resolve_atc
text: query Character scalar; drug name resolve. Case-insensitive. Examples: "aspirin", "atorvastatin". source Character; resolution strategy: "local" — bundled database (offline, fast) "live" — website (always --date, requires internet) "hybrid" — try local first, fall back live (default) data Optional data frame local search. NULL, auto-loads bundled database. fuzzy Logical; TRUE exact lookup fails, fall back fuzzy matching trying live resolution. Default TRUE. rate_limit Numeric; delay live requests (seconds). used source "live" "hybrid". Default 0.5.

title: Resolve a drug name to its ATC code with hybrid local/live lookup — resolve_atc
text: tibble columns: query — original query atc_code — resolved ATC code atc_name — drug name database source — "local" "live" indicating match came match_type — match found ("exact", "fuzzy", "live") Returns 0-row tibble drug resolved.

title: Resolve a drug name to its ATC code with hybrid local/live lookup — resolve_atc
